title: Alphabet raises 2026 capex ceiling to $205B, cloud revenue jumps 82%
subtitle: Q2 revenue beats but stock falls 5% after-hours as Google Cloud backlog swells to $514B and free cash flow turns negative.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Alphabet reported second-quarter revenue of $119.8 billion after the market close on July 22, beating consensus of $116.9 billion, with Google Cloud revenue up 82% year-over-year to $24.8 billion. In the same release the company raised its 2026 capital expenditure guidance to $195-205 billion, up from the $180-190 billion range set in April. Quarterly capex was a record $44.9 billion — more than double the year-ago figure — pushing free cash flow to negative $5.9 billion versus a positive $10.4 billion a year earlier. Alphabet shares fell about 5% in extended trading.

The market reaction is worth pausing on. Cloud grew 82%, backlog stands at $514 billion, earnings themselves beat — and the stock still sold off. The reason is capex intensity: investors are being asked to accept several years of negative or thin free cash flow while Alphabet builds out AI-specialized data centers, TPUs and interconnect. That is a coherent bet on the shape of AI infrastructure demand, but it changes the risk profile of the stock from an ad-revenue cash machine to something closer to a hyperscale infrastructure company.

The $514 billion cloud backlog matters more than the quarterly print. It is the largest disclosed forward book in the industry and it signals enterprise customers are locking in multi-year commitments for AI-adjacent compute. OpenAI raised its own compute projection to $750 billion through 2030 the same day. Meta, Microsoft and Amazon are all guiding to record 2026 capex. The four largest cloud buyers are collectively committing over $700 billion this year — a level of infrastructure buildout with no historical peer.

For learners: notice how the capex race changes what kinds of engineering skills are scarce. When four companies each buy hundreds of billions in data center capacity, the constraint is no longer chips or models — it is people who can design power systems, negotiate substation upgrades, run high-density liquid cooling, and coordinate multi-region networking. If you are entering the AI job market in 2026-2027, an ML-only resume competes with a large pool. A resume that combines ML fluency with data center operations, power engineering or network architecture competes with a much smaller pool for jobs that will exist regardless of which AI product wins.
